items.text:
🔍 *ENRICH REVIEW*  \[✨ ENRICHED\]

*Name:* Lochindorb Castle
*Country:* scotland
*Region:* highlands
*County:* Highland
*Type:* castle
*Period:* 13th century
*Coords:* 57.4075, -3.70638889
*Wikipedia:* https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lochindorb
*Maps:* https://www.google.com/maps/search/Lochindorb%20Castle%20Highland%20scotland
*Main Image:* https://lh3.googleusercontent.com/place-photos/AJRVUZO-6a97twJgHikGeedAORRqMPQDzYRbhrEMiCXUb2LC8kY5doiY2R-GBSXN3OpdtthAukQeFFN1wyZYjLBP7ZAKqoniTbv89nFmFg27-VpEXRulGyXr_2eAnBhtMYoKFwZADfmLdEdyxri1ZjM=s4800-w1200
*Gallery:* 0 images
*Image Source:* google
*Has Wikipedia:* unknown
*Has Google Places:* unknown

*Description:*
Lochindorb is a freshwater loch north of Grantown on Spey in the Highland council area of Scotland. In the loch there is an island, which is now thought to have been artificially created, and on that island are the ruins of Lochindorb Castle, a former stronghold of the Clan Comyn. King Edward I of England stayed in the castle during his 1303 campaign against the Scots. The loch is a popular spot with fishers, and also with birdwatchers, who come to see the local black-throated divers and greylag geese. At the southeast edge of the loch there is a small patch of coniferous forest.

_Listing 5 of 10_

listing.desc: Lochindorb is a freshwater loch north of Grantown on Spey in the Highland council area of Scotland. In the loch there is an island, which is now thought to have been artificially created, and on that island are the ruins of Lochindorb Castle, a former stronghold of the Clan Comyn. King Edward I of England stayed in the castle during his 1303 campaign against the Scots. The loch is a popular spot with fishers, and also with birdwatchers, who come to see the local black-throated divers and greylag geese. At the southeast edge of the loch there is a small patch of coniferous forest.

imageAttribution.description: Photograph of Lochindorb Castle, Badenoch, Scotland.

items.text:
🔍 *ENRICH REVIEW*  \[✨ ENRICHED\]

*Name:* Jarlshof
*Country:* scotland
*Region:* highlands
*County:* Shetland
*Type:* settlement
*Period:* Bronze Age to 17th century
*Coords:* 59.8693725, -1.2910305
*Wikipedia:* https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jarlshof
*Maps:* https://www.google.com/maps/search/Jarlshof%20Shetland%20scotland
*Main Image:* https://lh3.googleusercontent.com/places/ANXAkqF-V01PDQ_1zppYpVojUo8Sv7HQycnB8KXAlkNp2kl-ugq7W1Yk2qqi5uMmnyV9sr4m8rBWqXiJU-0GKPc-zdMP61zeOddocTw=s4800-w768
*Gallery:* 0 images
*Image Source:* google
*Has Wikipedia:* unknown
*Has Google Places:* unknown

*Description:*
Jarlshof is the best-known prehistoric archaeological site in Shetland, Scotland. It lies in Sumburgh, Mainland, Shetland and has been described as "one of the most remarkable archaeological sites ever excavated in the British Isles". It contains remains dating from 2500 BC up to the 17th century AD.

_Listing 7 of 10_

It contains remains dating from 2500 BC up to the 17th century AD.

items.text:
🔍 *ENRICH REVIEW*  \[✨ ENRICHED\]

*Name:* Clickimin Broch
*Country:* scotland
*Region:* highlands
*County:* Shetland
*Type:* broch
*Period:* Bronze Age / Iron Age
*Coords:* 60.149289, -1.165543
*Wikipedia:* https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Broch_of_Clickimin
*Maps:* https://www.google.com/maps/search/Clickimin%20Broch%20Shetland%20scotland
*Main Image:* https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ef/Clickimin_Broch_20080820_04.jpg/330px-Clickimin_Broch_20080820_04.jpg
*Gallery:* 0 images
*Image Source:* wikipedia
*Has Wikipedia:* yes
*Has Google Places:* unknown

*Description:*
The Broch of Clickimin is a large, well-preserved but restored broch in Lerwick, Shetland, Scotland. Originally built on an island in Clickimin Loch, it was approached by a stone causeway. The broch is situated within a walled enclosure and, unusually for brochs, features a large "forework" or "blockhouse" between the opening in the enclosure and the broch itself. The site is maintained by Historic Scotland. According to its excavator, John R.C. Hamilton, there were several periods of occupation of the site: Late Bronze Age farmstead, Early Iron Age farmstead, Iron Age fort, broch period, and wheelhouse settlement.

_Listing 8 of 10_

listing.name: Clickimin Broch
listing.desc: The Broch of Clickimin is a large, well-preserved but restored broch in Lerwick, Shetland, Scotland. Originally built on an island in Clickimin Loch, it was approached by a stone causeway. The broch is situated within a walled enclosure and, unusually for brochs, features a large "forework" or "blockhouse" between the opening in the enclosure and the broch itself. The site is maintained by Historic Scotland. According to its excavator, John R.C. Hamilton, there were several periods of occupation of the site: Late Bronze Age farmstead, Early Iron Age farmstead, Iron Age fort, broch period, and wheelhouse settlement.

items.text:
🔍 *ENRICH REVIEW*  \[✨ ENRICHED\]

*Name:* Mousa Broch
*Country:* scotland
*Region:* highlands
*County:* Shetland
*Type:* broch
*Period:* Iron Age (c. 100 BC)
*Coords:* 59.9952664, -1.1820277
*Wikipedia:* https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Broch_of_Mousa
*Maps:* https://www.google.com/maps/search/Mousa%20Broch%20Shetland%20scotland
*Main Image:* https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/81/Mousa_Broch_20080821_02.jpg/330px-Mousa_Broch_20080821_02.jpg
*Gallery:* 0 images
*Image Source:* wikipedia
*Has Wikipedia:* yes
*Has Google Places:* unknown

*Description:*
Broch of Mousa is a preserved Iron Age broch or round tower. It is on the island of Mousa in Shetland, Scotland. It is the tallest broch still standing and amongst the best-preserved prehistoric buildings in Europe. It is thought to have been constructed c. 300 BC, and is one of more than 500 brochs built in Scotland. The site is managed by Historic Environment Scotland as a scheduled monument.

_Listing 9 of 10_
